title: CLAUDE.md
text: file provides guidance Claude Code (claude.ai/code) working code repository.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: plotjs R package (previously c3plot) provides base R graphics-like interface Chart.js JavaScript charting library via htmlwidgets. Users can create interactive charts minimal syntax changes base R plotting calls.

title: CLAUDE.md
code: # Load package for interactive development devtools::load_all()  # Run all tests devtools::test()  # Run tests for a specific file testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test-plotjsScatter.R")  # Regenerate documentation (man/ and NAMESPACE) from roxygen2 comments devtools::document()  # Full package check (runs R CMD check) devtools::check()

title: CLAUDE.md
text: package two layers must stay sync:

title: CLAUDE.md
text: plotjsScatter.R — plotjs() S3 generic methods (default, density, function, lm), plus internal plotjsScatter() htmlwidget factory Shiny bindings jsbarplot.R — jsbarplot(), plotjs.factor() S3 method, Shiny bindings jspie.R — jspie() pie/donut charts Shiny bindings utils.R — col2hex() (R color name → hex) plotjsSizingPolicy() (htmlwidgets sizing wrapper) R function builds named list calls htmlwidgets::createWidget(). widget name must match .js file inst/htmlwidgets/.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: plotjsScatter.js, jsbarplot.js, jspie.js — individual HTMLWidgets bindings; receives serialized R list x configures Chart.js instance lib/plotjsWidget.js — shared utilities used three widgets: color palette, ensureChart/destroyChart/resizeChart, legendOptions, scientificTick, buildScatterDataset, pieLabelPlugin custom Chart.js plugin lib/chart.umd.min.js — Chart.js v4.5.1 (bundled, upgrade without testing chart types) lib/chartjs-plugin-zoom.min.js + lib/hammer.min.js — zoom/gesture support

title: CLAUDE.md
text: R serializes data → htmlwidgets::createWidget() → JSON → JS widget renderValue(el, x) → plotjsWidget.js helpers → Chart.js

title: CLAUDE.md
text: modify R source files (R/) unless explicitly asked. API stability primary goal. public interface intentionally mirrors base R graphics conventions (argument names, defaults, S3 dispatch patterns). new R code needed, follow style R’s graphics functions — terse argument names (col, lty, lwd, xlab, ylab, main), ... extensibility, S3 dispatch /else branching type.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: S3 dispatch: New plot types added plotjs.<class>() methods plotjsScatter.R, new top-level functions, unless chart type fundamentally different (bar, pie files use separate Chart.js types). Color handling: R color names converted hex via col2hex() passed JavaScript. Always use col2hex() accepting user color arguments. Documentation: exported functions use roxygen2 comments. Run devtools::document() editing @param/@export tags — never edit NAMESPACE man/ directly. Testing: Tests use testthat. Snapshot tests live tests/testthat/_snaps/. Test widget creation structure returned htmlwidget object, rendered output.

title: Benchmark
text: vignette benchmarks plotjs various plotting systems R, namely plotly. Two basic plots used benchmark, basic scatter plot grouped line plot. uncertain JavaScript execution time counted microbenchmark. Even assume isn’t, benchmark results informative ’s always better R process gets tied less time per plot. First, let’s load visualization packages compare:
code: library(plotjs) library(plotly) #> Loading required package: ggplot2 #>  #> Attaching package: 'plotly' #> The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2': #>  #>     last_plot #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter #> The following object is masked from 'package:graphics': #>  #>     layout library(ggplot2)

title: Benchmark
text: First, benchmark creation simple scatter plots using data gapminder package. begin, define functions create similar scatterplots using packages compared. plots important shown demonstrate work produce roughly similar plots.   Now, functions benchmarked: main development machine, plotjs quickest order magnitude. can vary, plotly roughly 20 times slower, ggplotly() hundreds times slower. However, plotly still quick enough performance difference plotjs imperceptible users.  Let’s look kernel density plots time distributions plotjs plotly. Let’s use two-sample Wilcoxon test compare means execution time plotjs plotly. t-test suitable assume normality. null hypothesis plotjs plotly mean execution time scatter plots. Can reject null hypothesis?
code: library(gapminder)  gapminder <- gapminder  plot_base <- function(x){      plot(x = x$gdpPercap, y = x$lifeExp) }  plot_base(gapminder) plot_plotjs <- function(x){     plotjs(x = x$gdpPercap, y = x$lifeExp, sci.x = TRUE) } plot_plotjs(gapminder) plot_plotly <- function(x){   plot_ly(data = x, x = ~gdpPercap, y = ~lifeExp, type = "scatter") } plot_plotly(gapminder) #> No scatter mode specifed: #>   Setting the mode to markers #>   Read more about this attribute -> https://plotly.com/r/reference/#scatter-mode plot_ggplotly <- function(x){   g <- ggplot(x, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) + geom_point() + theme_minimal()   ggplotly(g) } plot_ggplotly(gapminder) plot_ggplot <- function(x){   ggplot(x, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) + geom_point() + theme_minimal() } plot_ggplot(gapminder) library(microbenchmark) m <- microbenchmark(base = plot_base(gapminder),                plotjs = plot_plotjs(gapminder),                plotly = plot_plotly(gapminder),                ggplotly = plot_ggplotly(gapminder),                ggplot = plot_ggplot(gapminder),                unit = "ms",                times = 50) m #> Unit: milliseconds #>      expr        min         lq        mean      median         uq        max #>      base  23.629246  60.865074  60.5484762  61.0267845  61.658493  64.648799 #>    plotjs   0.072605   0.110847   0.1503552   0.1222180   0.134121   1.599622 #>    plotly   0.358719   0.398753   0.5135419   0.4903745   0.562178   2.064650 #>  ggplotly 163.402806 170.153523 179.5304335 177.1822535 182.841911 305.042786 #>    ggplot  33.996657  37.748793  40.6885139  39.7698110  41.947501  63.346439 #>  neval #>     50 #>     50 #>     50 #>     50 #>     50 plot(m) density_plotjs <- density(m$time[m$expr == "plotjs"]) plotjs(density_plotjs) density_plotly <- density(m$time[m$expr == "plotly"]) plotjs(density_plotly) w <- wilcox.test(m$time[m$expr == "plotjs"],                  m$time[m$expr == "plotly"],                  alternative = "less",                  paired = FALSE) w #>  #>  Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction #>  #> data:  m$time[m$expr == "plotjs"] and m$time[m$expr == "plotly"] #> W = 49, p-value < 2.2e-16 #> alternative hypothesis: true location shift is less than 0 ifelse(w$p.value < .05, "yes", "no") #> [1] "yes"

title: Benchmark
text: Making line plots colored group common plotting task potentially expose slowness plotjs. make line plots total GDP continent year. First, must summarize data define functions making lineplot various packages.  Now let’s benchmark line plot functions:  Let’s perform test : Can reject null hypothesis plotjs plotly mean?
code: library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union gdp_cont <- gapminder %>%   mutate(gdp = pop * gdpPercap) %>%   group_by(continent, year) %>%   summarize(total_gdp = sum(gdp)) #> `summarise()` has regrouped the output. #> ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by continent and year. #> ℹ Output is grouped by continent. #> ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message. #> ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(continent, year))` for per-operation grouping #>   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.  plot_title <- "Total GDP by Continent 1952 - 2007" plotjs_line <- function(x){   plotjs(x$year, x$total_gdp, col.group = x$continent, sci.y = TRUE,           type = "l", main = plot_title, xlab = "Year", ylab = "GDP",          legend.title = "Continent") }  plotjs_line(gdp_cont) ggplot_line <- function(x){   ggplot(x, aes(x = year, y = total_gdp, col = continent, group = continent)) +     geom_line() +     theme_minimal() +     labs(title = plot_title, x = "Year", y = "GDP") } ggplot_line(gdp_cont) ggplotly_line <- function(x){   p <- ggplot(x, aes(x = year, y = total_gdp, col = continent)) +     geom_line() +     theme_minimal() +     labs(title = plot_title, x = "Year", y = "GDP")   ggplotly(p) }  ggplotly_line(gdp_cont) plotly_line <- function(x){   plot_ly(data = x, x = ~year, y = ~total_gdp, split = ~continent,           type = "scatter", color  = ~continent, mode = "lines") } plotly_line(gdp_cont) m2 <- microbenchmark(plotjs = plotjs_line(gdp_cont),                      ggplotly = ggplotly_line(gdp_cont),                      plotly = plotly_line(gdp_cont),                      ggplot = ggplot_line(gdp_cont),                      unit = "ms",                      times = 50) m2 #> Unit: milliseconds #>      expr        min         lq        mean      median         uq        max #>    plotjs   0.436002   0.473974   0.5689041   0.5517940   0.584409   2.389476 #>  ggplotly 171.592587 175.660040 182.1086385 177.8061115 181.133736 312.348247 #>    plotly   0.340495   0.388815   0.5428028   0.4277025   0.531842   3.538517 #>    ggplot  35.541227  36.019188  37.9266177  36.5539200  39.219625  47.003569 #>  neval #>     50 #>     50 #>     50 #>     50 plot(m2) w2 <- wilcox.test(m2$time[m2$expr == "plotjs"],                  m2$time[m2$expr == "plotly"],                  alternative = "less",                  paired = FALSE) w2 #>  #>  Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction #>  #> data:  m2$time[m2$expr == "plotjs"] and m2$time[m2$expr == "plotly"] #> W = 1968, p-value = 1 #> alternative hypothesis: true location shift is less than 0 ifelse(w2$p.value < .05, "yes", "no") #> [1] "no"

title: Benchmark
text: Although benchmark results vary different systems, results development machine indicate plotjs faster plotly (others) scatter plot grouped line plot tested. Although statistically significant, difference performance plotjs plotly almost certainly never perceptible users. plotjs direct use plotly potentially offer perceptible performance improvements using ggplotly() generate interactive visualizations. Shiny developers may find information useful.

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: R renowned graphical capabilities, base R’s ‘graphics’ package makes easy quick create clean, useful plots. However, plots created base graphics static images therefore lack interactive features tooltips zooming. plotjs package implements various plotting functions similar names, arguments behavior counterparts base graphics, except plots create interactive HTML widgets rendered using Chart.js charting library. makes easy generate interactive versions common plots without cognitive overhead using learning plotting package completely different arguments syntax. vignette help get started creating interactive visualizations plotjs. first step install package:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("arkraieski/plotjs")

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: plotjs() function works much like base R’s plot() function. create scatter plot, simply provide two vectors (often data.frame) arguments x y. many optional arguments can specified, ’ll get later document. First, let’s create basic scatter plot using base R refresher:  Now plotjs(): can use mouse wheel zoom chart, points tooltips. Importantly, neither base R plot earlier much way chartjunk.
code: library(plotjs) mtcars <- mtcars # R's Motor Trend Cars Dataset  plot(x = mtcars$hp, y = mtcars$qsec) plotjs(x = mtcars$hp, y = mtcars$qsec)

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: Just like plot(), can specify plot title x y axis labels using arguments main, xlab, ylab, respectively:
code: plotjs(x = mtcars$hp, y = mtcars$qsec,        main = "1/4 Mile Time vs. Horsepower",        xlab = "HP",        ylab = "Seconds")

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: Like base plot() function, plotjs() can also create line plots setting type argument “l” (default “p” points). can also show points lines setting “b”.
code: x <- 1:10 y <- x*x  plotjs(x, y, type = "l", zoom = FALSE)

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: plotjs() colors points can specified using col argument. Colors can given strings containing hex value strings names R built-colors. can run grDevices::colors() see valid color names. color points color, provide single value col: Points can also colored groups data. two ways : Pass factor length x col.group argument leave col NULL. Points grouped col.group, group assigned color Chart.js using default palette. Pass factor col.group vector colors use group col. First, let’s color scatter plot factor let Chart.js pick colors: Now, coloring manually R:
code: plotjs(x = mtcars$hp, y = mtcars$qsec, col = "red") # convert mtcars$cyl to a factor mtcars$cyl <- as.factor(paste(mtcars$cyl, "cylinders"))  plotjs(mtcars$hp, mtcars$mpg, col.group = mtcars$cyl, legend.title = "cyl") plotjs(mtcars$hp, mtcars$mpg, col.group = mtcars$cyl,         col = c("red2", "yellow3", "purple"),        legend.title = "cyl")

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: Sometimes, boss might want make pie chart. plotjs also provides jspie() function works like base R pie function, albeit simplified set arguments. x vector non-negative numerical quantities. Names pie slices can given character vector labels argument. x named vector, names used label pie slices (labels defaults names(x)). ’s example adapted help file pie(): want something little better, can also use donut argument make donut chart:
code: pie.sales <- c(0.12, 0.3, 0.26, 0.16, 0.04, 0.12) names(pie.sales) <- c("Blueberry", "Cherry",     "Apple", "Boston Cream", "Other", "Vanilla Cream") jspie(pie.sales) jspie(pie.sales, donut = TRUE)

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: Like base’s plot(), plotjs() generic function S3 methods various classes base R objects. plotjs() doesn’t many methods, nonetheless provides useful shortcuts making common plots. see available methods, run: far, ’ve working default method. method help available. example, run ?plotjs.density view help file plotjs.density(). Let’s create density plot Old Faithful geyser data using plotjs.density(). First, create “density” object: Since plotjs() method “density” objects, can plot object simply passing x argument (method doesn’t need y): Compare base R version:  plots similar aside visual differences obviously interactivity plotjs version. plotjs() methods designed replicate functionality Base R counterparts. plotjs.function() lets us cool stuff like : (note use scientific notation y-axis):
code: methods(plotjs) #> [1] plotjs.default*  plotjs.density*  plotjs.factor*   plotjs.function* #> [5] plotjs.lm*       #> see '?methods' for accessing help and source code d <- density(faithful$eruptions, bw = "sj") class(d) #> [1] "density" d #>  #> Call: #>  density.default(x = faithful$eruptions, bw = "sj") #>  #> Data: faithful$eruptions (272 obs.); Bandwidth 'bw' = 0.14 #>  #>        x               y             #>  Min.   :1.180   Min.   :0.0001814   #>  1st Qu.:2.265   1st Qu.:0.0421820   #>  Median :3.350   Median :0.1706817   #>  Mean   :3.350   Mean   :0.2299476   #>  3rd Qu.:4.435   3rd Qu.:0.4130131   #>  Max.   :5.520   Max.   :0.5940669 plotjs(d) plot(d) plotjs(qnorm) # default range c(0, 1) plotjs(sin, -pi,  3*pi, col = "orange2", sci.y = TRUE)

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: package also provides jsbarplot() function modeled barplot() base graphics. Pass numeric vector describing heights bars heights argument. can also provide vector names bars argument names.arg (otherwise x-axis numbered sequentially). ’s simple, silly example: can also use “shortcut” provided plotjs.factor() method make bar chart factor level counts. Simply pass factor x argument:
code: jsbarplot(heights = c(100, 30), names.arg = c("Red Sox", "Yankees"), ylab = "% Awesome",       main = "Awesomeness of Baseball Teams") # cyl column of mtcars was already converted into a factor earlier in this vignette plotjs(mtcars$cyl, col = "#FF2800", main = "Cars by Number of Cylinders in mtcars")

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: plotting functions accept aria.label argument. provided, string set aria-label attribute chart’s <canvas> element, along role="img", screen readers can announce meaningful description chart. Without aria.label, canvas element label screen readers skip announce generically. good label describes chart’s main finding data shows.
code: plotjs(mtcars$hp, mtcars$qsec,        main = "1/4 Mile Time vs. Horsepower",        aria.label = "Scatter plot of 1/4 mile time vs. horsepower for 32 cars from the mtcars dataset.")  jsbarplot(heights = c(100, 30), names.arg = c("Red Sox", "Yankees"),           ylab = "% Awesome", main = "Awesomeness of Baseball Teams",           aria.label = "Bar chart comparing awesomeness: Red Sox 100%, Yankees 30%.")  jspie(pie.sales,       aria.label = "Pie chart of pie flavor sales: Cherry 30%, Apple 26%, Boston Cream 16%, Blueberry 12%, Vanilla Cream 12%, Other 4%.")

title: Getting Started with plotjs
text: now ready start using plotjs data. Consult help pages details arguments methods. plotjs doesn’t offer quite level customization base graphics, ’s simple, interactive alternative many common base plots.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Alex Kraieski. Author, maintainer. R Core team. Contributor.           code examples extracted base R 'plot()' methods

title: Authors and Citation
text: Kraieski (2026). plotjs: R 'graphics'-like Interface 'Chart.js'. R package version 0.3.0, https://arkraieski.github.io/plotjs/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {plotjs: An R 'graphics'-like Interface to 'Chart.js'},   author = {Alex Kraieski},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.3.0},   url = {https://arkraieski.github.io/plotjs/}, }

title: plotjs
text: goal plotjs provide base ‘graphics’-like interface Chart.js JavaScript charting library. main plotjs() function S3 generic like base plot() function methods various base R objects. Besides htmlwidgets, minimal R dependencies. Tidyverse required!

title: plotjs
text: can install development version GitHub :
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("arkraieski/plotjs")

title: plotjs
text: ’s example code basic scatter plot using R’s airquality dataset:
code: library(plotjs) air <- airquality plotjs(air$Ozone, air$Solar.R,         main = "New York Air Quality Measurements",         xlab = "Ozone (ppb)",        ylab = "Solar Radiation (lang)",        col = "red")

title: plotjs
text: see list S3 methods: way! goal replicate behavior base R plot() methods closely possible also getting benefits using Chart.js-backed interactive widget. method help page can accessed additional information.
code: library(plotjs) methods(plotjs) #> [1] plotjs.default*  plotjs.density*  plotjs.factor*   plotjs.function* #> [5] plotjs.lm*       #> see '?methods' for accessing help and source code

title: plotjs
text: package tested Windows, Linux, MacOS.

title: Shiny bindings for jsbarplot — jsbarplot-shiny
text: Output render functions using jsbarplot within Shiny applications interactive Rmd documents.

title: Shiny bindings for jsbarplot — jsbarplot-shiny
code: jsbarplotOutput(outputId, width = "100%", height = "400px")  renderJsbarplot(expr, env = parent.frame(), quoted = FALSE)

title: Shiny bindings for jsbarplot — jsbarplot-shiny
text: outputId output variable read width, height Must valid CSS unit (like '100%', '400px', 'auto') number, coerced string 'px' appended. expr expression generates jsbarplot env environment evaluate expr. quoted expr quoted expression (quote())? useful want save expression variable.

title: plotjs Bar Plots — jsbarplot
text: Creates bar plot using 'Chart.js'.

title: plotjs Bar Plots — jsbarplot
code: jsbarplot(   heights,   names.arg = NULL,   col = NULL,   main = NULL,   ylab = NULL,   width = NULL,   height = NULL,   elementId = NULL,   aria.label = NULL,   ... )

title: plotjs Bar Plots — jsbarplot
text: heights vector values describing bars make plot. names.arg vector names plotted bar. col character string color bars. can either hex value name R built-color. main main title plot. ylab label y axis. width width widget create plot. default NULL, results automatic resizing based plot's container. height height widget create plot. default NULL, results automatic resizing based plot's container. elementId Use explicit element ID widget, rather automatically generated one. aria.label character string set aria-label attribute chart's canvas element accessibility. ... arguments passed methods.

title: Shiny bindings for jspie — jspie-shiny
text: Output render functions using jspie within Shiny applications interactive Rmd documents.

title: Shiny bindings for jspie — jspie-shiny
code: jspieOutput(outputId, width = "100%", height = "400px")  renderJspie(expr, env = parent.frame(), quoted = FALSE)

title: Shiny bindings for jspie — jspie-shiny
text: outputId output variable read width, height Must valid CSS unit (like '100%', '400px', 'auto') number, coerced string 'px' appended. expr expression generates jspie env environment evaluate expr. quoted expr quoted expression (quote())? useful want save expression variable.

title: plotjs Pie Charts — jspie
text: Draw interactive pie chart using 'Chart.js'

title: plotjs Pie Charts — jspie
code: jspie(   x,   labels = names(x),   col = NULL,   slice.text = "pct",   donut = FALSE,   legend.position = "right",   main = NULL,   width = NULL,   height = NULL,   elementId = NULL,   aria.label = NULL )

title: plotjs Pie Charts — jspie
text: x vector non-negative numerical quantities. values x displayed areas pie slices. labels character vector giving names slices. col character vector colors used filling slices. Can hex value R built-color name. slice.text "pct" display percentage-formatted values inside pie slices "id" display slice's name labels. donut logical; chart rendered donut chart instead full pie? legend.position Position legend. Possible values "right", "bottom", "inset", "hide". main main title plot. width width widget create plot. default NULL, results automatic resizing based plot's container. height height widget create plot. default NULL, results automatic resizing based plot's container. elementId Use explicit element ID widget, rather automatically generated one. aria.label character string set aria-label attribute chart's canvas element accessibility.

title: plotjs Pie Charts — jspie
code: pie.sales <- c(0.12, 0.3, 0.26, 0.16, 0.04, 0.12) names(pie.sales) <- c("Blueberry", "Cherry",                     "Apple", "Boston Cream", "Other", "Vanilla Cream") jspie(pie.sales)  {"x":{"labels":["Blueberry","Cherry","Apple","Boston Cream","Other","Vanilla Cream"],"values":[0.12,0.3,0.26,0.16,0.04,0.12],"colors":null,"main":null,"label_mode":"pct","donut":false,"legend":{"position":"right","hide":false},"aria_label":null},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}jspie(pie.sales, col = c("purple", "violetred1", "green3",                      "cornsilk", "cyan", "white"))  {"x":{"labels":["Blueberry","Cherry","Apple","Boston Cream","Other","Vanilla Cream"],"values":[0.12,0.3,0.26,0.16,0.04,0.12],"colors":["#A020F0","#FF3E96","#00CD00","#FFF8DC","#00FFFF","#FFFFFF"],"main":null,"label_mode":"pct","donut":false,"legend":{"position":"right","hide":false},"aria_label":null},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}jspie(pie.sales, donut = TRUE)  {"x":{"labels":["Blueberry","Cherry","Apple","Boston Cream","Other","Vanilla Cream"],"values":[0.12,0.3,0.26,0.16,0.04,0.12],"colors":null,"main":null,"label_mode":"pct","donut":true,"legend":{"position":"right","hide":false},"aria_label":null},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}

title: Shiny bindings for plotjs — plotjs-shiny
text: outputId output variable read width, height Must valid CSS unit (like '100%', '400px', 'auto') number, coerced string 'px' appended. expr expression generates plotjsScatter env environment evaluate expr. quoted expr quoted expression (quote())? useful want save expression variable.

what: The Default plotjs Scatterplot Function — plotjs.default
title: The Default plotjs Scatterplot Function — plotjs.default
text: Draw interactive scatterplot line plot using 'Chart.js'.

title: The Default plotjs Scatterplot Function — plotjs.default
code: # Default S3 method plotjs(   x,   y,   type = "p",   xlim = NULL,   ylim = NULL,   main = NULL,   xlab = NULL,   ylab = NULL,   zoom = TRUE,   col.group = NULL,   col = NULL,   legend.title = NULL,   legend.position = "right",   sci.x = FALSE,   sci.y = FALSE,   ... )

title: The Default plotjs Scatterplot Function — plotjs.default
text: x x coordinates plot. y y coordinates plot. type 1-character string giving type plot desired. following values possible: "p" points, "l" lines, "b" points lines. xlim x limits (x1, x2) plot. ylim y limits plot. main main title plot. xlab label x axis, defaults description x. ylab label y axis, defaults description y. zoom logical; zooming feature enabled plot? col.group optionally, factor length x group color points. col colors lines points. col.group specified, can vector colors use group data. NULL, Chart.js default colors used. legend.title title legend. Defaults description col.group set. can also use legend.title = FALSE suppress legend title. legend.position Position plot legend. Possible values "right", "bottom", "inset", "hide". ignored points colored . sci.x logical indicating whether scientific notation used x-axis. sci.y logical indicating whether scientific notation used y-axis. ... arguments passed htmlwidgets::createWidget(): width, height, elementId. arguments default NULL. may also pass aria.label, character string set aria-label attribute chart's canvas element accessibility.

title: The Default plotjs Scatterplot Function — plotjs.default
